title: APUSH stimulus-based MCQ archetype catalog (agent-iterable)
note: Companion to docs/01_apush_question_taxonomy.md. On the AP U.S. History exam EVERY Section I Part A multiple-choice question is stimulus-based: a source (primary text, secondary/historian text, image/cartoon, map, or chart/table) is followed by a SET of 3-4 questions. A concrete item = (stimulus) x (historical-thinking skill / reasoning process) x (stem template) x (period+theme content). Families collapse the College Board practices + reasoning processes into generation buckets; archetypes are the named, generation-ready stem shapes an agent iterates over. The decisive generation levers are two CLOSED menus: (1) stem_templates (the command phrase, which signals the skill) and (2) distractor_traps (the four ways College Board makes a wrong answer plausible). 'expert_feel' and 'note_seedability' are 0-1 design heuristics for scope selection, not College Board data. 'anachronism_verifiable' flags archetypes whose answer key is checkable against a date table.

section_1a_mcq.form: sets of 3-4 questions per shared stimulus
section_1a_mcq.note: stimulus is ALWAYS present; ~every item couples source-interpretation with outside knowledge

section_1b_saq.form: 3 short parts (a,b,c); Q1 secondary source(s), Q2 primary source, Q3/Q4 no stimulus

section_2a_dbq.form: 7 documents; write a thesis-driven, evidence-based argument

section_2b_leq.form: choose 1 of 3; thesis-driven essay; one reasoning process

reason_frq_out_of_scope: SAQ/DBQ/LEQ are open-ended written answers graded by rubric, not machine-checkable single-best-answer items; see docs/01b for how the same skill spine maps to them.

historical_thinking_skills.name: Developments and Processes
historical_thinking_skills.essence: identify and explain historical developments and processes

historical_thinking_skills.name: Sourcing and Situation
historical_thinking_skills.essence: analyze point of view, purpose, historical situation, and audience of a source

historical_thinking_skills.name: Claims and Evidence in Sources
historical_thinking_skills.essence: analyze the argument of a source and what evidence supports or undermines it

historical_thinking_skills.name: Contextualization
historical_thinking_skills.essence: situate a source/event in the broader historical context that produced or surrounds it

historical_thinking_skills.name: Making Connections
historical_thinking_skills.essence: apply a reasoning process (comparison / causation / continuity-and-change) across developments

historical_thinking_skills.name: Argumentation
historical_thinking_skills.essence: develop and defend a historical argument (FRQ-facing; not a single-best-answer MCQ skill)

reasoning_processes.name: Causation
aspects: explain relationship of causes and effects
distinguish primary vs secondary causes and short- vs long-term effects
explain how context influenced a development

reasoning_processes.name: Continuity and Change
aspects: describe patterns of continuity/change over time
explain relative significance within a larger pattern

content_axis.periods_note: Core periods 3-8 are ~80% of the exam; ranges intentionally overlap (College Board periodization).

stimulus_types.note: speech, law, letter, platform, court opinion, treaty, pamphlet, diary; author is a historical actor. Richest soil for sourcing + causation.
historian's interpretation; enables 'support/undermine the argument' and 'competing interpretations' — the crown-jewel argument-evidence items.
political cartoon, poster, photograph; for a TEXT-ONLY SLM the image must be represented as a caption + prose description (see docs/01b).
settlement, expansion, election, migration maps; text-only SLM uses a described map.
demographic/economic time-series; the 'pattern -> most direct cause' item lives here.

stem_templates.phrase: Which of the following contributed most directly to <the development in the source>?
stem_templates.note: answer is a CAUSE; keyed option must predate the stimulus.

stem_templates.phrase: The <action/idea> in the excerpt most immediately led to ...
... most directly contributed to which of the following later characteristics/developments?
stem_templates.note: answer is a LONG-TERM effect; borders on CCOT.

stem_templates.phrase: The <source/image> most directly reflects / best illustrates which of the following developments?
stem_templates.note: answer is the broader development the source is an instance of.

stem_templates.phrase: The <remarks/source> were most likely given in response to which of the following?
stem_templates.note: answer is the situation that prompted the source.

stem_templates.phrase: The ideas expressed in the excerpt were most directly influenced by ...
The <source/image> was most likely intended to ...
The author's point of view is best described as / would most likely agree with ...
stem_templates.note: answer is the stance implied by the source.

stem_templates.phrase: Which of the following could best be used as evidence to support the argument in the <source>?
stem_templates.note: answer is an outside development that strengthens the source's claim. CROWN JEWEL.

stem_templates.phrase: Which of the following could best be used as evidence to challenge/undermine the argument in the <source>?
stem_templates.note: answer is an outside development that weakens the source's claim. CROWN JEWEL.

stem_templates.phrase: Which of the following best serves as evidence for the claim that <quoted claim>?
stem_templates.note: answer is the specific fact that grounds a quoted sub-claim.

stem_templates.phrase: Which of the following later developments had an effect most similar to that described in the excerpt?
stem_templates.note: answer is a cross-time analog. Comparison + CCOT.

stem_templates.phrase: In <later decade>, the <activity/idea> would be most challenged by / most continued by ...
stem_templates.note: answer is a later development that breaks or extends the pattern.

stem_templates.phrase: The view in the excerpt differs from <other group/source> most significantly in that ...
stem_templates.note: answer is the axis of difference between two positions.

distractor_traps.note: THE core generation lever. College Board makes wrong answers plausible using exactly four architectures; every distractor an expert item uses is one of these, and each maps to a nameable error. An LLM's tell is filler distractors that fit none of these. Every generated distractor MUST be labeled with its trap id.

menu.rule: a real development from a DIFFERENT time period than the stimulus/answer requires
historically accurate for the era but does NOT answer the specific stem (wrong theme/mechanism)
menu.student_error: picks a familiar true statement without checking it answers THIS question

menu.rule: too broad (general background when the stem asks 'most directly'/specific mechanism) or too narrow (a single example when the stem asks for a trend)
menu.student_error: matches topic but not the scale/directness the command phrase demands

menu.rule: one clause accurate, one clause fabricated/overstated (often joined by 'and'/'while'), or right direction with wrong magnitude
menu.student_error: endorses the accurate half, misses the false half

distractor_traps.distribution_rule: In a 4-option item the 3 distractors should span >=2 distinct trap types (never 3 copies of the same trap); at least one distractor should be the hardest available trap for that stem (usually true_but_irrelevant or scope_mismatch, which require real era knowledge to reject).

families.name: Comprehension & Sourcing
families.essence: read what the source says/is: its main point, its author's POV/purpose/audience, and the development it exemplifies

families.name: Contextualization
families.essence: connect the source to the broader situation that produced it or that it reflects

families.name: Causation
families.essence: identify the most-direct cause or effect of the development in the source

families.name: Continuity, Change & Comparison
families.essence: relate the development across time (continuation/change, later analog) or to a parallel development

families.name: Argument & Evidence
families.essence: evaluate a (usually secondary) source's ARGUMENT: what outside evidence would support or undermine it; compare rival interpretations

archetypes.input_needed: a stimulus that is a concrete instance of a broader labeled development/process
archetypes.shape: give the source; ask which broader development it best illustrates/reflects; distractors are other developments of the same era (true_but_irrelevant) or adjacent eras (wrong_era)

good_example.stimulus: Larson (historian, 2001) on the Erie Canal enriching New York and spurring rival seaports to seek westward connections.
good_example.stem: The excerpt best illustrates which of the following developments?
good_example.answer: The expansion of access to markets (the Market Revolution)
distractors_rationale: extension of commerce with Native Americans (true_but_irrelevant: wrong theme)
growth in the internal slave trade (true_but_irrelevant: real era, wrong mechanism)
increase in semisubsistence agriculture (partially_true: opposite of the market integration described)
good_example.source: AP U.S. History CED, Sample Exam Q5 (analysis only, not training data)

archetypes.llm_failure: the 'development' just paraphrases the source (no outside label), or distractors are absurd/other-century so topic-matching gives it away

archetypes.input_needed: a source with a discernible authorial goal / audience (persuasive primary source or propaganda image)
archetypes.shape: ask what the author most likely intended / whom they addressed / their stance; distractors are goals of adjacent movements (true_but_irrelevant) or later movements (wrong_era)

good_example.stimulus: 1940s Boeing wartime poster depicting women in aircraft production.
good_example.stem: The image was most likely intended to promote
good_example.answer: the movement of women into jobs traditionally held by men
distractors_rationale: federal civil rights legislation (wrong_era / true_but_irrelevant)
belief that women should have equal rights (scope_mismatch: broader ideology, not the poster's wartime-labor purpose)
union membership regardless of race (true_but_irrelevant: wrong theme)
good_example.source: AP U.S. History CED, Sample Exam Q12 (analysis only)

archetypes.llm_failure: purpose is stated verbatim in the caption; distractors are not real period goals
difficulty_levers: how close the decoy purposes are

archetypes.input_needed: a source that is a clear RESPONSE to a specific prior situation/crisis
archetypes.shape: ask what the source was most likely responding to; correct answer is the antecedent situation; distractors are wrong-era crises or contemporaneous-but-irrelevant events

good_example.stimulus: President Ford, Remarks on Taking the Oath of Office, 1974 ('our long national nightmare is over').
good_example.stem: The remarks in the excerpt were most likely given in response to which of the following?
good_example.answer: Political scandals resulting from the president's illegal campaign activity (Watergate)
distractors_rationale: wage/price-control 'socialism' accusations (true_but_irrelevant)
antiwar protests / Kent State (wrong_era: 1970, and wrong cause)
Cambodia/Laos invasions (true_but_irrelevant: real but not what prompted the oath)
good_example.source: AP U.S. History CED, Sample Exam Q15 (analysis only)

archetypes.llm_failure: the prompting event is named in the stimulus; distractors are not plausible same-era alternatives

archetypes.input_needed: a source expressing an idea with an identifiable intellectual/movement antecedent
archetypes.shape: ask which prior idea/movement most directly shaped the source's ideas; distractors are later movements (wrong_era) or same-era-but-unrelated movements (true_but_irrelevant)

good_example.stimulus: Republican Party platform, 1860, denying authority to give 'legal existence to slavery in any territory.'
good_example.stem: The ideas expressed in the excerpt were most directly influenced by the
distractors_rationale: nativist movement (true_but_irrelevant: same era, wrong idea)
Texas independence movement (true_but_irrelevant / scope_mismatch)
temperance movement (true_but_irrelevant: reform era, wrong domain)
good_example.source: AP U.S. History CED, Sample Exam Q9 (analysis only)

archetypes.llm_failure: the influence is a synonym of the source's own words; distractors are from unrelated centuries

archetypes.input_needed: a source describing a development that has an identifiable most-direct cause
archetypes.shape: ask what contributed MOST DIRECTLY to the development; the trap is a broad background condition vs the specific mechanism (scope_mismatch); distractors include a wrong-era cause and a true-but-irrelevant cause

good_example.stimulus: The Declaratory Act, passed by the British Parliament in 1766 (asserting authority to bind the colonies 'in all cases whatsoever').
good_example.stem: Which of the following contributed most directly to the enactment of the law in the excerpt?
good_example.answer: Debates over how Britain's colonies should bear the cost of the Seven Years' War
distractors_rationale: increasing cultural divergence (scope_mismatch: broad background, not the direct fiscal cause)
drafting a declaration of independence (wrong_era: after 1766)
conflicts over Native sovereignty (true_but_irrelevant)
good_example.source: AP U.S. History CED, Sample Exam Q1 (analysis only)

archetypes.llm_failure: restates the source as its own cause; offers no 'broad background vs direct mechanism' trap; distractors not date-consistent
difficulty_levers: presence of a tempting background-condition distractor
primary vs chart stimulus (chart adds 'explain the pattern')

archetypes.input_needed: a source describing a development with an identifiable short- or long-term effect
archetypes.shape: ask what the development most immediately/directly led to; correct answer is the effect; distractors are wrong-era effects, reversed-direction effects (partially_true), or true-but-unconnected later events

good_example.stem: The actions described in the excerpt most immediately led to
good_example.answer: Parliament strengthening its approach to generating new tax revenue in the colonies (e.g., Townshend Acts)
distractors_rationale: violent conflict between colonial regions (partially_true: conflict yes, but colonists vs Britain, not region vs region)
a colonial convention calling for independence (wrong_era: premature)
Britain delegating greater authority to assemblies (partially_true: opposite direction)
good_example.source: AP U.S. History CED, Sample Exam Q2 (analysis only)

archetypes.llm_failure: the 'effect' is another synonym for the source; the reversed-direction distractor is obviously false

archetypes.input_needed: a source whose debates/ideas shaped a durable later feature of US government/society
archetypes.shape: ask which LATER characteristic/development the source's debates most directly contributed to; spans causation into CCOT; distractors are other real later features unconnected to this source

good_example.stimulus: The Declaratory Act, 1766, and colonial debates over parliamentary authority.
good_example.stem: Debates over the claims of the British Parliament in the excerpt most directly contributed to which of the following later characteristics of the United States government?
good_example.answer: The reservation of some governmental powers for the states (federalism)
distractors_rationale: presidential term limits (true_but_irrelevant: later, unconnected)
taxation power in Congress (partially_true: related tension, but the Act's legacy is limits on central power, not granting it)
judicial review (true_but_irrelevant: different origin, Marbury)
good_example.source: AP U.S. History CED, Sample Exam Q4 (analysis only)

archetypes.llm_failure: picks the most famous later feature regardless of causal link; no distractor forces cause-tracing
difficulty_levers: how many real later features compete

archetypes.input_needed: a source describing a development with a characteristic effect that recurs later in US history
archetypes.shape: ask which LATER development had an effect MOST SIMILAR to the one described; correct answer shares the underlying mechanism/effect; distractors share surface topic but not the effect (true_but_irrelevant) or are wrong-era

good_example.stimulus: Larson (historian) on the Erie Canal's market integration and interregional competition.
good_example.stem: Which of the following later developments had an effect most similar to that described in the excerpt?
good_example.answer: The completion of the first transcontinental railroad in the 1860s
distractors_rationale: mechanical reaper, 1830s (true_but_irrelevant: raised farm output, not interregional market integration)
annexation of Texas, 1840s (true_but_irrelevant: wrong theme)
party competition of the 1850s (true_but_irrelevant: wrong theme)
good_example.source: AP U.S. History CED, Sample Exam Q7 (analysis only)

archetypes.llm_failure: matches on topic keyword instead of shared effect/mechanism; distractors not real later developments
difficulty_levers: how abstract the shared mechanism is
how tempting the surface-topic decoys are

archetypes.input_needed: a source describing a pattern that is later extended OR reversed
archetypes.shape: ask what LATER development would most challenge (change) or most continue the pattern; correct answer is the later break/extension; distractors are wrong-direction or wrong-era

good_example.stimulus: 1940s image of women in wartime industrial jobs.
good_example.stem: In the 1950s, the activities of workers such as those depicted would be most challenged by
good_example.answer: a culture of conformity that emphasized domestic ideals for women
distractors_rationale: need for dual incomes in suburbs (partially_true: coexisted, doesn't 'challenge' wartime work role)
second-wave feminism (wrong_era: 1960s, and would extend not challenge)
decline of industrial production (wrong_era: long-term, later)
good_example.source: AP U.S. History CED, Sample Exam Q14 (analysis only)

archetypes.llm_failure: confuses 'continues' with 'challenges'; distractor directions not distinct
difficulty_levers: how adjacent the decoy decades are

archetypes.input_needed: a source (often SECONDARY) making an explicit ARGUMENT that outside evidence could strengthen
archetypes.shape: ask which outside development best SUPPORTS the source's argument; correct answer is period-consistent and logically strengthens the specific claim; distractors are era-plausible facts that support a DIFFERENT claim (true_but_irrelevant) or that actually cut the other way (partially_true)

good_example.stimulus: Larson (historian), 2nd paragraph: rival seaports feared 'permanent eclipse' and scrambled for westward connections.
good_example.stem: Which of the following developments could best be used as evidence to support the argument in the second paragraph?
good_example.answer: The opposition of some political leaders to providing federal funds for public works (reflecting the interregional rivalry the author describes)
distractors_rationale: failure of some projects to recover costs (true_but_irrelevant: about viability, not rivalry)
recruitment of immigrant laborers (true_but_irrelevant: labor, not rivalry)
rise of a cotton export economy (scope_mismatch: regional economy, not the canal-rivalry claim)
good_example.source: AP U.S. History CED, Sample Exam Q6 (analysis only)

archetypes.llm_failure: any era-appropriate fact is offered as 'support' with no test of whether it strengthens the SPECIFIC claim; no distractor that supports a neighboring claim
difficulty_levers: how narrowly the claim is scoped

archetypes.input_needed: a source making an argument that outside evidence could weaken
archetypes.shape: ask which outside development best CHALLENGES the argument; correct answer logically weakens the specific claim; distractors support it (partially_true reversal), are irrelevant, or are wrong-era

good_example.stimulus: A historian argues that Reconstruction failed primarily because Northern voters lost interest by the mid-1870s.
good_example.stem: Which of the following could best be used to challenge the historian's argument?
good_example.answer: The scale of white-supremacist terrorism and the Supreme Court's narrowing of the 14th Amendment, which undercut enforcement regardless of Northern opinion
distractors_rationale: the Compromise of 1877 withdrawing troops (partially_true: consistent WITH the argument, not against)
passage of the 15th Amendment (true_but_irrelevant: earlier achievement)
Plessy v. Ferguson, 1896 (wrong_era: post-Reconstruction formalization)
good_example.source: constructed to shape; grounded in docs/01 trap-cluster research

archetypes.llm_failure: offers a fact that CONFIRMS the argument as the 'challenge'; cannot distinguish support from undermine
difficulty_levers: how subtle the confirm-vs-challenge distinction is
presence of a tempting confirming distractor

archetypes.input_needed: TWO secondary sources (or a source + a stated rival view) on the same development
archetypes.shape: ask how the two interpretations most significantly differ, or which evidence one historian would use against the other; correct answer names the true axis of disagreement; distractors name a point of agreement (partially_true) or a difference not actually present (fabricated)

good_example.stimulus: Peiss (1986): dance halls 'legitimized' working women's public leisure. Enstad (1999): the 1909 mass strike made working women political actors.
good_example.stem: The interpretations differ most significantly over which of the following?
good_example.answer: Whether working women's public presence was primarily cultural/consumer or primarily political
distractors_rationale: whether working women were active in public (partially_true: both agree they were)
whether the women were immigrants (true_but_irrelevant: not the axis of dispute)
whether the period was 1880-1929 (fabricated non-difference)
good_example.source: adapted from AP U.S. History CED SAQ secondary-source pair (analysis only)

archetypes.llm_failure: states a difference both historians actually share; or invents a disagreement not in the sources
difficulty_levers: how close the two interpretations are

overlays.rule: stimulus is a described political cartoon/poster/photo (caption + prose description); the answer must follow from the depicted content + era, not the caption alone
overlays.failure: caption gives away the answer; description is too vague to ground any option

overlays.rule: stimulus is a described data table/time-series; the correct answer must be forced by the described trend (e.g., a sharp post-1921 drop), turning causation into 'explain the pattern'
overlays.failure: answerable from memory without the described trend

overlays.rule: generate 3 non-redundant items on ONE stimulus, each a different skill/stem_template (e.g., cause + reflects + similar_effect), never re-testing the same fact
overlays.failure: two items key the same development or paraphrase each other

overlays.rule: stimulus is a historian's ARGUMENT (dated to publication, cited); items test the argument, not the historian's era
overlays.failure: treats the historian's publication date as the historical period being tested

quality_checks.rule: every item has a stimulus (text/described image/described chart); the stem references it; the item is NOT answerable as free-floating trivia
the correct answer requires connecting the source to a development NOT stated in the source (the APUSH analog of 'familiar concept, unfamiliar scenario'); paraphrase-only items are rejected
each of the 3 distractors is labeled with exactly one distractor_traps.menu id and that label is defensible
each distractor is a real, era-plausible development a good-but-imperfect student would consider; no absurd/anachronism-obvious or off-topic filler
the 3 distractors span >=2 distinct trap types
the answer is the KIND of thing the stem_template demands (cause stem -> a cause; support stem -> a strengthener); reject answers that are merely 'related and true'

if archetype.anachronism_verifiable, the keyed answer's development date is consistent with stem_templates.answer_time_direction relative to the stimulus date (cause=before, effect=after); a wrong-era distractor must violate it
exactly one option fully answers the stem; others are clearly inferior; use 'most directly/best' framing when >1 is partly defensible
all four options are the same category (all developments, or all purposes, or all responses...), similar length/grammar; correct answer not the longest
reject options with always/never/all-of/none-of; reject absolute-word tells
a knowledgeable reader can state what is being asked from the stem+stimulus before reading options

generation_loop: 1. select a stimulus from stimulus_corpus_ref (legally-sourced primary/secondary text, or a described image/chart), tagged with period + theme + date
2. select an archetype (weighted by the Deliverable-3 scope decision) whose input_needed the stimulus satisfies
3. instantiate the archetype's stem_template against the stimulus (fill the command phrase)
4. derive the correct answer from (source claim) + (one outside development from developments_table_ref consistent with answer_time_direction)
5. author 3 distractors, each drawn from the distractor_traps menu, spanning >=2 trap types; label each with its trap id and the named error
6. self-filter against quality_checks (drop on any disqualifying failure); run anachronism_key_check where applicable
7. optionally apply SET_OF_THREE to produce a full stimulus set of 3 non-redundant items
8. keep survivors; log the (stimulus_id, archetype, period, theme, trap_types) tuple for coverage tracking
